claim: Verified: YOLO demand evidence dispatch implements boundary audit that detects tracked and untracked target-project mutations in git-backed and non-git projects via git ls-files and filesystem walk fallback.

evidence.summary: Implementation chain from gitFiles (ls-files -co) through walkFiles (recursive walk) to buildBoundarySnapshot (union with exclusions) covers all acceptance criteria requirements.
evidence.why: Core implementation matches problem statement: detects tracked and untracked mutations in both git-backed and non-git projects.

evidence.summary: diffBoundarySnapshots uses SHA256 digests to detect added, modified, and deleted files between snapshots.
evidence.why: Change detection mechanism is content-addressed and reliable for mutation detection.

evidence.summary: Pre-execution snapshot at line 590 and post-execution comparison at lines 640-646 integrate boundary audit into agent execution flow.
evidence.why: Evidence that boundary audit is not just implemented but actually executed during agent runs.

evidence.summary: Three test scenarios cover git-backed untracked mutations, gitignored files, and non-git projects with artifact exclusion.
evidence.why: Test coverage validates implementation claims against acceptance criteria.

evidence.summary: Boundary mutations populate BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers in readiness gating.
evidence.why: Evidence that detected mutations actually block PRD readiness as required.

risks: Non-standard excluded directories beyond walkFiles hardcoded list may not be covered.
Concurrent agent executions may create race conditions in snapshot timing.
Create-then-delete patterns within single execution may evade endpoint-based detection.

result.notes: All evidence records are properly scoped with valid repo-relative paths and line numbers. No assumptions presented as facts. Implementation matches acceptance criteria: git ls-files for tracked/untracked, walkFiles fallback for non-git, artifact root exclusion, and PRD readiness blocking on violations. Test coverage confirms behavior across git-backed and non-git scenarios. Explorer risks acknowledged but do not block current PRD readiness.
